mnemonics.title: RIP Timers Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: UIF - Update (30s), Invalid (180s), Flush (240s). Remember: Update-InvaliD-Flush. U = 30, I = 180, F = 240. Just multiply by 30: 1x30=30, 6x30=180, 8x30=240.

mnemonics.title: RIP Maximum Hops
mnemonics.content: 15 HOP = 1-5. Think: RIP = R-I-P = 1-5 in alphabetical positions. R=18, I=9, P=16... Wait! Just remember: RIP has 15 hops - think 15 letters in 'RoutingInformation' or 'Protocol' isn't it? No! Just remember: RIP = 1 + 5 = 15.

mnemonics.title: Administrative Distance
mnemonics.content: RIP AD = 120. Remember: RIP = 120 - 'RIP' sounds like '1-20' (one-twenty).

mnemonics.title: RIPv1 vs RIPv2
mnemonics.content: RIPv1: Classful, No VLSM, Broadcast. RIPv2: Classless, VLSM, Multicast. Remember: V1 = Very Basic, V2 = Very Better. Or: 1=No, 2=Yes for classless features.

acronyms.usage: Interior gateway protocol using distance-vector algorithm
Classful version of RIP, no VLSM support
Classless version with VLSM, CIDR, and multicast support
Allows subnets of different sizes within the same network
IP addressing method replacing classful addressing
Connectionless transport protocol used by RIP
Hop limit in IP packets to prevent infinite loops

visualTips.visualization: Imagine three circles: Small (30s update), Medium (180s invalid), Large (240s flush). They overlap forming a timeline. When smallest fires, medium counts down, then largest cleans up.
Draw two arrows between two routers A and B. Route goes A→B→A→B... Each arrow adds +1 until it hits 16 (∞). Like bouncing a ball that gains energy each bounce.
Think of a mirror at each interface. If route came FROM interface, it bounces BACK - but split horizon says DON'T reflect it back. Mirror blocks the reflection.
Draw a road with a poison symbol at the end (metric 16). When route becomes unreachable, it's 'poisoned' - no one can use that road anymore.

analogies.analogy: Like a town crier walking around every 30 seconds shouting the entire news (routing table) to everyone. Even if nothing changed!
Like counting intersections to reach a destination. More intersections = longer path. RIP only cares about how many turns, not road quality.
Like asking neighbors 'How far is the library?' They answer based on what they heard from THEIR neighbors. Each person only knows direction and distance.
Like grapevine in an office. Bad news (route failure) spreads slowly through whispers. By the time everyone knows, information may be outdated.
Think of RIP's metric as a 4-bit counter. It can only count 0-15. 16 is the overflow - it means 'too far to count' = unreachable.
